incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1: would require 70 tick marks, far too many for readable graph

params.description: Scale of 1 option highlights with '70 doesn't fit' warning indicator.

steps.text: Scale of 1 would need 70 tick marks to reach Ben's total. That's way too many lines on the axis. Look at the biggest number: 70. Which scale reaches 70 without too many marks?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2: would require 35 tick marks, still too many for data up to 70

params.description: Scale of 2 option highlights with '70 doesn't fit' warning indicator.

steps.text: Scale of 2 would need 35 tick marks to reach 70. That's still too many. For bigger numbers, try a bigger scale. Which scale reaches 70 with fewer marks on the axis?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5: would require 14 tick marks, better but Scale of 10 more efficient for data this large

params.description: Scale of 5 option highlights with '70 doesn't fit' warning indicator.

steps.text: Scale of 5 could work, but it needs 14 tick marks to reach 70. For data up to 70, there's an even cleaner choice. Which scale needs the fewest marks?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System demonstrates tick mark counts: Scale of 1 shows 70 marks, Scale of 2 shows 35 marks, Scale of 5 shows 14 marks, Scale of 10 shows 8 marks. Scale of 10 highlighted as best choice.

steps.text: Let me show you. The biggest number is 70. Scale of 1 needs 70 tick marks. Scale of 2 needs 35. Scale of 5 needs 14. But Scale of 10 only needs 8 tick marks to reach 70. That's the cleanest graph. The answer is Scale of 10.
